questions.question: Explain the FIFO principle in queues and give a real-life example.
keyPoints: FIFO stands for First-In-First-Out
Elements added first are removed first
Real-life example: People standing in a ticket line, first person gets ticket first
Used in scheduling, print queues, BFS algorithms

questions.question: What is the difference between queue overflow and queue underflow? Explain with conditions.
keyPoints: Overflow: Attempting to insert into a full queue
Underflow: Attempting to delete from an empty queue
Overflow condition: rear >= MAXSIZE - 1
Underflow condition: front > rear or front == -1

questions.question: Describe the two primary operations of a queue with their time complexity.
keyPoints: Enqueue: Insert element at rear - O(1) time
Dequeue: Remove element from front - O(1) time
Both operations are constant time because they only modify pointer positions
Requires checking for overflow before enqueue and underflow before dequeue

questions.question: What are the four essential operations supported by a queue data structure?
keyPoints: enqueue (or push): Insert at rear
dequeue (or pop): Remove from front
front (or peek): View front element without removal
isEmpty/isFull: Check queue status

questions.question: Explain the front deletion problem in linear queues.
keyPoints: In linear queue, after dequeue, front space cannot be reused
After many operations, rear reaches maximum even when queue has empty spaces
This leads to false overflow condition
Solution is to use circular queue

questions.question: What is a circular queue? How does it solve the memory wastage problem?
keyPoints: Circular queue connects front and rear in a circular fashion
Uses modulo arithmetic to wrap around
When rear reaches end, it wraps to beginning
Allows reuse of vacated spaces, eliminating front deletion problem

questions.question: Write an algorithm to check if a circular queue is empty. Also mention the condition for full circular queue.
keyPoints: Empty condition: front == -1 and rear == -1 (or front == rear == -1)
Full condition: (rear + 1) % MAXSIZE == front
In circular queue, one space is kept empty to distinguish full from empty

questions.question: Explain the array implementation of a queue with diagrams. Include initialization, enqueue, dequeue, and full/empty conditions.
keyPoints: Array of fixed size with front and rear pointers
Initialize: front = rear = -1
Enqueue: Check overflow, increment rear, insert at rear
Dequeue: Check underflow, retrieve front element, increment front
Empty: front == -1 or front > rear
Full: rear == MAXSIZE - 1
Discuss disadvantages leading to circular queue

questions.question: Explain the circular queue implementation with pseudo code for enqueue and dequeue operations. Also analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Circular queue wraps around using modulo operation
Enqueue: Check full using (rear+1)%MAXSIZE == front
Dequeue: Check empty using front == -1
After dequeue: front = (front+1)%MAXSIZE
After enqueue when empty: front = rear = 0
Time complexity: O(1) for both operations
Space complexity: O(n) where n is queue size

questions.question: Discuss at least five real-world applications of queue data structure in computer science.
keyPoints: CPU scheduling: Process scheduling in operating systems
BFS traversal: Graph traversal uses queue level by level
Print queue: Managing print jobs in order they are submitted
Task queues: Managing asynchronous tasks in web servers
Customer service: Call center systems managing waiting customers
Transaction processing: Banking systems process transactions in order
IoT buffer: Managing data packets in network communication

questions.question: Write a C program to implement a queue using array with all basic operations. Also explain how circular queue overcomes the limitations of linear queue.
keyPoints: Structure definition with array and pointers
Initialize, isFull, isEmpty functions
Enqueue: Add at rear, increment rear
Dequeue: Remove from front, increment front
Linear queue limitation: Cannot reuse front space after dequeue
Circular queue: Wraps around using modulo, reuses empty spaces
Comparison of both approaches
